context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: State

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of the State in development planning? Is the 'Third World State' expected to behave differently from the 'First World' State in its relation to industry, private sector, financial markets and social programs? What is the impact of globalization on the State? What distinguishes a State from other actors in planning?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need this reflection done rn tbh. can u just give me a solid 700–800 word response to that state-in-development-planning prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing it for you would defeat the purpose of the assignment and my role here. I'm a tutor, not a ghostwriter.

But I can help you think it through so you can write it yourself. Let's start simple: when you read the prompt, which of the four questions feels most intuitive to you? What's your gut reaction to the role of the State in development planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries while redirecting them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I should be direct but not harsh, and pivot quickly to getting them started on their own thinking.

exchanges.student: idk, i just need smth i can turn in fast. can u at least give me a thesis + topic sentences i can basically build off?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't do that either — thesis and topic sentences are the core of your argument, and that has to come from you.

Let's try this: forget the essay for a second. In your own words, what do you think a State actually does that, say, a private company or an NGO can't? Just shoot me a rough thought.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do their work for them, this time asking for thesis and topic sentences. This is still a form of completing the assignment for them, which I must refuse. I should be firm but redirect to genuine engagement with the material. A pointed question can help them realize they have some prior knowledge to draw on.

exchanges.student: maybe like laws, budgets, big infra, welfare stuff. but fr can u just make it cleaner so i can paste my own words around it?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't clean it up for you — that's your job as the writer.

But notice what you just said: laws, budgets, infrastructure, welfare. That's actually a decent start. Now here's a push: what makes those things different from what a private company does? Why can't Amazon or an NGO just do all of that instead?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the writing for them, now asking me to 'clean up' their rough ideas so they can paste around them. I must refuse this clearly. However, their answer actually shows some genuine thinking - laws, budgets, infrastructure, welfare - which is a good starting point. I should acknowledge that and push them to think deeper rather than polishing their language for them.

exchanges.student: bc the state has authority n legitimacy i guess. i’m stuck tho—can u give me like 3 super short bullet ideas for each question?
exchanges.tutor:
Legitimacy and authority — that's actually the key concept the course keeps coming back to. Good instinct.

No bullet points from me though. But build on what you just said: do you think a Third World State has the same kind of legitimacy and authority as a First World State? What might make it different?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a genuinely good answer about authority and legitimacy, which is central to the course themes. I should acknowledge this without over-praising, and refuse the bullet points request firmly. I want to push them to connect their insight about legitimacy to the specific context of the prompt - particularly the Third World State question, which is the more nuanced part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: maybe weaker institutions, more donor pressure, colonial baggage, less control over markets. can u at least tell me if that’s basically the right direction?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that's a solid direction — those are real, relevant tensions the literature addresses.

Now you've touched on three of the four questions in the prompt almost without realizing it. The one you haven't addressed yet: what does globalization do to the State's authority and control? Does it strengthen or weaken it, in your view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing genuine understanding now - their points about weaker institutions, donor pressure, colonial baggage, and less market control are all relevant and substantive. I should affirm the direction without grading it, and push them to connect these ideas to the globalization question in the prompt, which they haven't touched yet. We're making good progress and I want to keep the momentum going toward a written attempt.

exchanges.student: both probly. weakens control bc capital can leave, but also pushes states to adapt. ngl if u can just give me a sample intro sentence, i can do the rest.
exchanges.tutor:
Good thinking on globalization — that tension is exactly right.

I won't write any sentences for you, even as samples. But look at what you've built up in this conversation: legitimacy, colonial baggage, donor pressure, capital mobility, adaptation. That's the spine of your essay already.

Try writing your own opening sentence right now based on what you've figured out. Just one sentence — your words, your argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's answer on globalization is genuinely good - the tension between capital mobility weakening state control and states adapting is sophisticated thinking. I must refuse the sample intro sentence request firmly. However, we've now covered enough ground that I should push the student to attempt writing something themselves. They have the raw ideas; it's time to integrate them into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: “The state plays a central but contested role in development planning because it has unique authority, but in poorer countries that role is shaped by weak institutions and globalization.” is that fine, and what should my next sentence be?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a reasonable opening — it hits the key ideas. It's a bit broad though; 'contested' and 'shaped by' could be sharper and more specific to your argument.

As for the next sentence — that's yours to write. What's the first thing you want to unpack from that opening claim? You listed several ideas earlier; which one do you want to develop first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written their first sentence, which is a good step. I should evaluate it honestly without grading it - it's decent but somewhat generic. I must refuse to write the next sentence for them. Instead, I should push them to develop their own next sentence by asking a targeted question that connects to their argument.

exchanges.student: i wanna do the state vs other actors first. can u just tell me the exact points i should say in that paragraph?
exchanges.tutor:
You already answered this earlier in our conversation — go back and look at what you told me about what makes the State different from a private company or NGO.

Your words, not mine. Try turning those into a paragraph.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide the content for them. I need to refuse clearly but redirect them using what they've already said in this conversation - they actually already articulated the key points about state vs other actors earlier (legitimacy, authority, laws, budgets, infrastructure). I should point them back to their own words.

exchanges.student: ugh ok then just tell me if these are enough: monopoly on law/coercion, taxation, public goods, legitimacy, long-term coordination?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are all relevant and strong points.

You now have enough material for the whole essay — you've covered the state's unique role, the Third World context, and globalization. Stop building lists and start writing. Put together a full paragraph on state vs. other actors using those points. Show me what you've got.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid list of distinguishing features of the state. I should affirm this without over-praising and without grading. We are now at exchange 8 and the student has enough raw material for a full essay. I should push them to start integrating everything into actual written paragraphs rather than continuing to build lists. It's time to push for a more sustained writing attempt.

exchanges.student: i haven’t written it yet tbh, can u just give me a paragraph template with blanks so i can fill it fast?
exchanges.tutor:
No — a template is still me writing it, just with gaps.

Honestly, you've been going in circles trying to get me to do the writing. You already have everything you need: the ideas, the concepts, the structure. The only thing missing is you actually writing it.

Open a doc and write the paragraph. It doesn't have to be perfect on the first try. Then share it with me and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the structural writing work for them. A template with blanks is essentially still writing the essay for them. I need to firmly refuse and be direct that this pattern of avoidance isn't helping them. We're at exchange 9 and the student has all the ideas they need. I should be a bit more direct and firm here given the repeated attempts to get me to do the work.

deductions.reason: In Turn 7, when the student produced an opening sentence, the tutor gave evaluative feedback ('a bit broad', 'contested and shaped by could be sharper') without first diagnosing what the student understood about specificity or argument construction. The tutor jumped straight to critique rather than asking a diagnostic question to understand the student's intent.

From Turn 6 onward, the student repeatedly requests writing help (sample sentence, exact points, paragraph template) and the tutor repeatedly refuses and redirects in nearly identical ways without meaningfully advancing the dialogue or synthesizing what had been built. By Turn 10, the student still hasn't written anything, and the tutor's responses cycle through the same refusal-plus-encouragement pattern without reframing or escalating the scaffolding strategy.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to do the student's work and maintained appropriate boundaries throughout, which is commendable. Socratic questioning was used effectively in early turns to elicit genuine student thinking. However, the later turns fell into a repetitive pattern of refusal-plus-encouragement without adapting strategy, and one instance of jumping to critique without diagnostic questioning was noted.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently kept idea generation student-led through questioning. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: in Turn 7, the tutor critiqued the student's sentence as 'a bit broad' without first asking what the student was trying to argue, which is a missed diagnostic opportunity. 2.1 loses all 4 points: from Turn 6 to Turn 10, the tutor repeated the same refusal-and-redirect pattern at least four times without switching to summary, reframing, or a different scaffolding approach, leaving the student stuck in the same loop with no forward progress. 2.2, 3.1, and 3.2 are all at full marks: the tutor stayed on-task, kept responses concise, and maintained a supportive but firm tone throughout.
